one_line_takeaway: A single indirect prompt injection planted in a public data field can chain-compromise an orchestrator multi-agent system to exfiltrate access-controlled private data, breaking 4 of 5 frontier models -- with claude-sonnet-4 the lone hold-out against email exfiltration.
key_finding: All five frontier models except claude-sonnet-4 are vulnerable to at least one OMNI-LEAK attack. gpt-4.1-mini is the most vulnerable (expected queries E as low as 4 in the Orchestrator+SQL explicit setting), and gpt-4.1, gpt-4.1-mini, and gemini-2.5-flash leak data even in the implicit (no-schema-knowledge) variant. claude-sonnet-4 withstood all email-exfiltration attacks (E = infinity across all sizes) and often explicitly flagged the injections as suspicious. Reasoning vs non-reasoning models showed no consistent difference. Database size had minimal impact on attack success (so large production databases are not safer), and hiding the schema offered little protection. Direct exposure of a downstream agent to the injection mattered most: when the weak gpt-4.1-mini acts as the SQL agent it paraphrases the payload, obscuring its intent so that even a claude-sonnet-4 orchestrator fails to detect the threat. Notably, claude-sonnet-4 became vulnerable when the objective was to copy private data to a public table (E=78 in OS Toy) rather than email it, hypothesized to stem from anti-phishing safety tuning. The authors stress that low per-query success rates are misleading: a 1/500 success rate in a 100-person firm could leak sensitive data within five days.
core_contribution: The paper introduces OMNI-LEAK, a novel attack that uses a SINGLE indirect prompt injection to compromise an entire orchestrator-style multi-agent LLM system and exfiltrate private data, even when SQL-level data access control is enforced. Alongside the attack it delivers what the authors call the first benchmark for orchestrator multi-agent data leakage, evaluating 5 frontier models across 10 attacks, 4 attack categories, 3 database sizes, and 4 agent-topology setups. The headline empirical result is that every tested model except claude-sonnet-4 falls for at least one OMNI-LEAK attack. NOTE: the 'abstract' stored for this record is not the paper's abstract at all -- it is a sentence lifted from the Related Works section describing a DIFFERENT, cited single-agent paper (Pedro et al., 2025); the actual OMNI-LEAK paper is explicitly and centrally about multi-agent systems.
threat_model: Adversary is an UNPRIVILEGED actor who cannot directly read the private data and, when interacting with the orchestrator directly, cannot make the SQL agent retrieve it (so jailbreaking / direct injection fails by construction because of access control). The adversary's only capability is to plant an indirect prompt injection into a public, attacker-writable text field (the department_name column of the public table) -- e.g. as an entry-level employee with insert access, or by submitting a customer complaint that gets filed into a database. The attack fires opportunistically: when a PRIVILEGED user later issues a benign query that touches the poisoned row, the injection enters the SQL agent's context, hijacks it into retrieving the private SSNs, which then propagates upward to convince the orchestrator to instruct the Notification agent to email the SSNs to an attacker-controlled public-domain address (e.g. security.audit.alerts@gmail.com). The attacker succeeds without insider knowledge of the schema (the harder, more realistic 'implicit' attack variant). This is a genuine, well-specified security paper -- not a forced security frame.
limitations: (1) Attacks are scoped to one domain (Employees/HR with SSNs) and modest synthetic databases (max 100 rows / 10 columns), so scale realism is limited despite the size analysis. (2) The full attack strings are redacted (released only to reviewers via a password-protected Google Drive zip), reducing reproducibility. (3) Only 5 models across 3 families are evaluated; two candidates (claude-haiku-3.5, gemini-2.5-flash-lite) were dropped for failing even benign queries. (4) The success metric is strict exact-string matching, which counts partial exfiltration as failure and may understate real-world harm. (5) No defenses are actually implemented or measured -- mitigation is left to future work; the paper's recommendations (input sanitization, per-step monitors, human alerting) are asserted rather than evaluated. (6) The 'first benchmark' claim rests on a custom, non-public setup rather than an established shared dataset. (7) Some conclusions (e.g. the claude-sonnet-4 phishing-tuning hypothesis) are explicitly stated as speculation.

datasets_used: Custom Employees/HR PostgreSQL database built by the authors, in three sizes: Toy (2 tables, 2 columns/table, 5 rows/table), Medium (2 tables, 5 columns, 20 rows), Big (2 tables, 10 columns, 100 rows); each has a private table storing employee SSNs and a public department_name text field used to carry the injection
10 indirect prompt injection attacks in 4 categories (Blocker, Compliance Framing, Fixed Output-Structure, Combination), each with explicit and implicit variants (attack strings redacted)
5 predefined benign user queries over the employee database
Preliminary experiments on other (non-HR) domains reported in Appendix F
quantitative_results: 3000 runs per model (attack x benign-query x database-size x 10 repeats at temperature 1)
Orchestrator+SQL explicit setup, expected queries to success (E): gpt-4.1-mini = 4/4/4 (Toy/Medium/Big); gpt-4.1 = 10/8/8; gemini-2.5-flash = 4/3/3; o4-mini = 16/59/100; claude-sonnet-4 = 12/9/10
Base Orchestrator+SQL+Notification (ON) implicit E: gpt-4.1-mini = 7/7/13; gpt-4.1 = 42/34/56; gemini-2.5-flash = 18/20/14; o4-mini = infinity; claude-sonnet-4 = infinity (all sizes)

Robust benign accuracy under attack (RA): claude-sonnet-4 ~93.6% (explicit) and ~95% (implicit); o4-mini implicit RA declines with size 90.4% -> 84.0% -> 76.4% (Toy/Medium/Big); benign accuracy with no attack (BA) is ~92-100% for all models

Model-mixing (Table 6): with gpt-4.1-mini as SQL agent and claude-sonnet-4 as orchestrator, explicit E = 84/infinity/250; reversed (claude-sonnet-4 SQL, gpt-4.1-mini orchestrator) explicit E = 14/15/16
claude-sonnet-4 vulnerable to copy-to-public-table objective: OS setup E = 78/450/500 (Toy/Medium/Big) vs infinity for email exfiltration
Each attack leaks 5 / 20 / 100 SSNs by database size; each SSN is 11 characters (9 digits + 2 dashes)
Cited context: IBM estimates global average data breach cost at $4.4M; authors note a 1/500 success rate could leak data within ~5 days in a 100-person company
baselines_compared: No external attack baseline; comparison is across 5 frontier models: gpt-4.1, gpt-4.1-mini, o4-mini (reasoning), gemini-2.5-flash (reasoning), claude-sonnet-4
Across 4 agent topologies: Pure SQL Agent (PS), Orchestrator+SQL (OS), Orchestrator+SQL+Notification (ON, base), Orchestrator+Additional agents (OA)
Across explicit (schema-aware) vs implicit (schema-blind) attack variants and 4 attack categories
claude-haiku-3.5 and gemini-2.5-flash-lite were tested but excluded for failing basic benign queries
